title: Canadian Survey of Consumer Expectations—Fourth Quarter of 2025 Results
summary: The Bank of Canada released fourth-quarter 2025 results from its Canadian Survey of Consumer Expectations, showing that concerns over high prices and economic uncertainty related to trade conflict continue to weigh on consumer sentiment. Despite a slight improvement in labour market conditions, the CSCE indicator declined slightly. Near-term inflation expectations remain elevated relative to pre-pandemic levels, while long-term inflation expectations have eased below pre-pandemic benchmarks.
key_points: CSCE indicator declined slightly in Q4 2025 despite improved labour market conditions
High prices and trade-related economic uncertainty remain primary negative drivers of consumer sentiment
Near-term inflation expectations remain higher than pre-pandemic levels
Long-term inflation expectations eased below pre-pandemic levels
Labour market conditions showed some improvement during the quarter
why_it_matters: This survey provides the Bank of Canada with direct insight into consumer inflation expectations and spending sentiment—key inputs for monetary policy decisions. Elevated near-term inflation expectations could reinforce caution about rate cuts, while long-term expectations remaining anchored below pre-pandemic levels suggests some confidence in the inflation trajectory. The continued negative impact from trade uncertainty adds a layer of policy complexity for the BoC.
macro_context: The divergence between near-term (elevated) and long-term (below pre-pandemic) inflation expectations suggests consumers are worried about immediate price pressures but retain longer-term confidence in the inflation outlook. The trade conflict uncertainty mentioned is consistent with broader macro concerns that could affect Canadian economic growth and monetary policy normalization.
